topic: Component 1: Personal Investigation (Practical and Written Element)
subject: art-design

items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: What is the first step of formal analysis?
items.correct: Description - noting what is actually present in the work.
items.why: Students jump to meaning immediately. Formal analysis follows a sequence: describe, analyse formal elements, interpret, evaluate. Description grounds interpretation in what's actually there.

items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.q: What is the typical word count range for the A-Level personal investigation written element?
items.why: Correct answer: 1000-3000 words. “100-500 words” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What term describes research drawn from books, articles and online sources?
items.why: Correct answer: Secondary research. “Primary research” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the maximum word count for the A-Level personal investigation written element under most exam boards?
items.why: The real figure is 3000 words. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
